topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into a quiet Tuesday evening. My heart gave a sudden, heavy lurch against my ribs. I clicked the link, my fingers trembling slightly over the keyboard. There it was: a listing for 14 Willow Creek Lane. My childhood home. A cold wave washed over me, stealing the air from my lungs. I reread the details, the familiar layout of the old garden sketched in the photos, and a knot tightened in my stomach. Who? Why now? I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the small apartment. I paced the room, running a hand over my face, trying to steady the frantic drumming beneath my skin. Every shadow in the room seemed to stretch and deepen, morphing into something menacing.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam before my eyes. The address—my old street—burned itself into my vision. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I started checking the listing again, my gaze darting from the property description to the contact information. Each word felt weighted, loaded with unspoken history. My hands began to sweat, slicking my palms as I gripped the edge of the desk. I stood up abruptly, needing space, needing to move away from the glowing rectangle. The silence of the room suddenly felt oppressive, thick with anticipation. I backed away slowly, my eyes scanning the darkened windows, certain that something unseen was watching from the periphery.

[story 3]

When the email arrived, the paper felt brittle in my hands. The address was unmistakable. A tremor started low in my core, vibrating outward through my legs. I sat down heavily on the sofa, the springs groaning in protest. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I kept glancing toward the front door, half-expecting to see someone materialize in the hallway. The details of the listing—the precise measurements, the photographs of the porch swing—seemed hyper-real, almost accusatory. I pushed the paper away, my throat suddenly dry. I stood up and walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just an inch, peering into the unfamiliar street. The world outside seemed too loud, too sharp, and I felt profoundly unsettled, rooted to the spot.
